title: Segregation of Primary Schools in Hungary. A Descriptive Study Using Data from the National Assessment of Basic Competences of 2006
summary_en: Descriptive analysis of school segregation in Hungarian primary schools using the National Assessment of Basic Competences (OKM) 2006 micro-data. Documents very high between-school Roma segregation that increased through the 2000s, with the segregation increase driven not by demographic shifts but by school-choice patterns — Roma students are increasingly concentrated in a small set of schools as non-Roma families exit.
policy_relevance: Direct input for the Hungarian Education Ministry on the school-segregation question. Demonstrates that without active de-segregation policy (school-catchment rules, integrated-class incentives), Roma school segregation increases mechanically through parental choice. Caveat: 2006 data; the church-school expansion since 2010 has further shifted patterns and the analysis would need updating.
